title: Solution of the Equation-of-Motion Phonon Method eigenvalue problems on the D-Wave quantum annealer
authors: C. De Lucia, A. Martone, F. A. D'Aniello, A. Mastroianni, G. Nunziata, G. De Gregorio, R. Folprecht, F. Knapp, N. Lo Iudice, P. Vesely
abstract: The solution of large-scale eigenvalue problems is crucial in nuclear many-body theory, where Hamiltonian matrices often reach extremely large dimensions. Quantum computing opens new perspectives for addressing such demanding problems. Although the Quantum Phase Estimation algorithm offers, in principle, a systematic route to matrix diagonalization, its practical deployment demands levels of coherence and error correction that current quantum hardware cannot yet support. A viable near-term strategy is instead to exploit quantum annealing, which enables the recasting of eigenvalue problems into quadratic unconstrained binary optimization formulations that can be addressed by existing annealing-based processors. Here, we propose a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm that combines quantum annealing and classical deflation to iteratively extract the full eigenspectrum of both standard and generalized eigenvalue problems. We benchmark this method on eigenvalue problems arising from the Equation of Motion Phonon Method performing calculations on real quantum hardware. Our approach illustrates the capabilities and limitations of near-term quantum devices in addressing nuclear eigenvalue problems.
summary: This work addresses the challenge of solving massive eigenvalue problems from nuclear physics models using current quantum hardware. It proposes a hybrid approach that encodes the problem into a QUBO solvable by quantum annealing, which is then combined with classical matrix deflation techniques to reconstruct the entire spectrum. This demonstrates a viable path for near-term quantum computation in fundamental many-body physics.
main_problem: The core problem is solving large-scale eigenvalue problems arising from nuclear many-body theory, which are computationally intractable for exact diagonalization.
main_result: A hybrid quantum-classical algorithm combining quantum annealing with classical deflation was proposed and benchmarked to iteratively extract the full eigenspectrum of both standard and generalized eigenvalue problems.
method: The continuous eigenvalue problem is mapped onto a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) formulation, which is then solved using quantum annealing. Full spectrum recovery relies on sequential application of matrix deflation techniques.
model_or_system: The method is applied to eigenvalue problems derived from the Equation-of-Motion Phonon Method (EMPM), modeling nuclear structure dynamics in a multiphonon basis.
key_observables:
Full eigenspectrum ($\{\Psi_
u\}$), eigenvalues ($E$), and eigenvectors, along with convergence behavior and eigenvalue accuracy.
important_parameters: Matrix dimensions ($d$), bit resolutions ($b=2, 4$), and the spectral shift parameter used in deflation.
main_assumptions: Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) is too demanding for current NISQ devices; thus, quantum annealing on QUBO formulations is used as a near-term alternative.
figures_summary: Figures compare accuracy ($\epsilon_p$) and total execution time ($	ext{tet}$) of different deflation methods (Hotelling, Orthogonal Projection, Householder) when solving SEVP/GEVP using both Simulated Annealing and Quantum Annealing.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical problem (EMPM eigenvalue equations), details the mapping to QUBO for quantum annealing, outlines iterative refinement via initial guess and descent phases, and finally implements full spectrum reconstruction using classical deflation techniques combined with quantum optimization steps.
why_interesting: It directly tackles a major computational bottleneck in nuclear structure theory by proposing a concrete, near-term quantum algorithm (QA on QUBO) to solve complex many-body eigenvalue problems that are central to understanding quantum dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Digits of accuracy of the lowest eigenvalue and of the corresponding eigenvector for the TDA Hamiltonian diagonalization with Jπ = 0+ and b = 2, performed with SA (a,c,e) and with QA (b,d,f). The value εp = −log10(precision) is also reported.
FIG. 2: Same as Fig. 1, for b = 4.
FIG. 3: Results for the solution of the GEVP within the Equation-of-Motion Phonon Method with Jπ = 0+, 1−, 2+
